title: Diving Deeper on NVIDIA's Vera CPU: New Architectural Details and SPEC CPU 2026 Benchmarks
gist: NVIDIA released its full Olympus/Vera CPU architectural whitepaper alongside new GPU and NVLink disclosures, revealing a conventional-but-wide, out-of-order core built around spatial multithreading, memory renaming, value prediction, and a novel graph prefetcher, with SPEC CPU 2026 benchmarks showing performance that is 'not all rosy' for NVIDIA's first bid to be a general-purpose server CPU vendor.

themes.theme: Strategic context: NVIDIA's push into general-purpose server CPUs

insights.claim: This week NVIDIA released a long-awaited GPU architecture tech blog, new NVLink interface details, and a full architectural whitepaper on its Vera CPU -- its deepest technical disclosure yet since the products were first 'launched' at the start of 2026 -- timed to get ahead of Intel's earnings season and AMD's Advancing AI event happening the same week.
insights.speaker: Ryan Smith
insights.so_what: The disclosure timing itself is a competitive signal -- NVIDIA moved first to frame the week's server-hardware narrative before Intel and AMD's own events.
insights.locator: Opening paragraphs on this week's disclosures

insights.claim: Vera represents NVIDIA's first custom CPU core design in many years and is the most interesting part of the Vera Rubin product stack, because it marks NVIDIA moving from Grace's role as a GPU companion chip toward wanting Vera used broadly across many kinds of servers.
insights.speaker: Ryan Smith
insights.so_what: Signals NVIDIA's ambition to become a standalone server-CPU vendor, not just a GPU company that also ships a companion chip.
insights.locator: NVIDIA Vera Exposed: Olympus CPU Architecture

insights.claim: NVIDIA wants Vera to win customers on its own merits while also dissuading buyers from pairing Rubin GPUs with a competitor's CPU, and as a result Vera is getting more promotional and architectural support from NVIDIA than its other product lines.
insights.speaker: Ryan Smith
insights.so_what: Explains NVIDIA's disclosure strategy: heavy Vera marketing is partly defensive, aimed at locking Rubin GPU sales to NVIDIA's own CPU platform.

insights.claim: The server CPU market is a very large, very lucrative, and highly competitive space -- with Intel, AMD, and numerous Arm-based CPU vendors already established -- making it arguably a harder market for NVIDIA to climb than GPUs or networking, where NVIDIA is already the world's largest vendor.
insights.speaker: Ryan Smith
insights.so_what: Tempers the assumption that NVIDIA's GPU dominance will automatically translate into CPU market share -- CPUs are a tougher fight.

insights.claim: Vera roughly doubles Grace's threading and cache resources: 88 Olympus cores vs. Grace's 72 Arm Neoverse V2 cores, 176 threads via Spatial Multithreading vs. 72, 2MB L2 cache per core vs. 1MB, and a 164MB unified L3 vs. 114MB.
insights.speaker: Ryan Smith
insights.so_what: Quantifies the generational jump in core count and cache hierarchy that server buyers will weigh against Intel/AMD offerings.

insights.claim: Vera's memory subsystem jumps to up to 1.2TB/s bandwidth and 1.5TB of LPDDR5X capacity, versus Grace's up to 512GB/s and 480GB, while NVLINK-C2C doubles from 900GB/s to 1.8TB/s and PCIe/CXL moves from Gen5 to Gen6/CXL 3.1.
insights.speaker: Ryan Smith
insights.so_what: Massive memory bandwidth/capacity and interconnect gains position Vera for memory-hungry AI and HPC workloads, not just as a GPU host chip.

insights.claim: Vera's SIMD width grows to 6x 128-bit SVE2 with FP8 support (from Grace's 4x 128-bit SVE2 without FP8), and TDP scales up to a 250-450W range versus Grace's flat 250W.
insights.speaker: Ryan Smith
insights.so_what: The added FP8 support and wider power envelope show Vera is explicitly tuned for AI workloads, trading power headroom for throughput.

themes.theme: Olympus core philosophy: wide and conventional, not radical

insights.claim: The Olympus core architecture is not wildly different from other high-end contemporary CPUs -- it is explicitly not like NVIDIA's earlier Denver core -- with NVIDIA choosing to build a better CPU within established design paradigms rather than pursue an unconventional approach.
insights.speaker: Ryan Smith
insights.so_what: Signals a strategic shift from NVIDIA's earlier VLIW-style Denver experiment toward a safer, execution-proven OoO design likely to be easier for software/toolchains to support well.
insights.quote: this is not Denver, and rather than thinking outside of the box NVIDIA’s overall direction is to build a better CPU that follows contemporary CPU design paradigms

insights.claim: Olympus's instruction fetch unit can feed up to 16 64-bit instructions into a 48-instruction decode queue, which in turn can emit up to 10 fused instructions per cycle into the decoder.
insights.speaker: Ryan Smith
insights.so_what: A very wide front end for a first-generation custom core, indicating NVIDIA is targeting high single-thread throughput.

insights.claim: Olympus has a 64KB, 4-way L1 instruction cache -- twice the size of AMD's Zen 5 L1 instruction cache and matching Intel's Cougar Cove -- alongside a 96KB, 6-way L1 data cache, underscoring NVIDIA's intent to handle large instruction footprints well.
insights.speaker: Ryan Smith
insights.so_what: A concrete, comparable data point against AMD's and Intel's latest cores that signals Olympus is designed for instruction-heavy, large-footprint workloads.
insights.quote: this is two-times the size of Zen 5’s L1 cache

insights.claim: Olympus can rename up to 10 micro-ops per cycle to keep pace with its decoder, though NVIDIA has not disclosed the size of its reorder buffer or register files used for renaming.
insights.speaker: Ryan Smith
insights.so_what: Confirms the mid-core is sized to match the wide front end, though key capacity metrics remain undisclosed, limiting full performance comparison.

insights.claim: Olympus uses memory renaming -- a superset of register renaming that renames whole memory addresses, also used in recent AMD designs -- to let dependent instructions execute before a load even completes, so long as the data relationship can be predicted.
insights.speaker: Ryan Smith
insights.so_what: Shows NVIDIA adopting a proven, AMD-pioneered ILP technique rather than inventing an unproven alternative, aiding confidence in the design's soundness.

insights.claim: Olympus also implements value prediction (speculatively guessing repeated instruction result values before they are generated, then verifying them) and move elimination (removing unnecessary memory-value movement instructions) to further boost instruction-level parallelism.
insights.speaker: Ryan Smith
insights.so_what: Additional advanced speculative-execution techniques that push Olympus toward the upper end of current OoO core sophistication.
insights.locator: Value prediction and move elimination description

themes.theme: Backend execution width and spatial multithreading

insights.claim: Each Olympus core has 18 execution pipes -- 8 integer/branch pipes, 6 vector/FP pipes, 4 load pipes, and 2 store pipes -- and all of these pipes exist in pairwise configurations, which is a key structural element enabling NVIDIA's partition-based spatial multithreading.
insights.speaker: Ryan Smith
insights.so_what: Reveals the physical design trick (paired pipes) underlying how one Olympus core presents as two logical threads -- key to Vera's 176-thread count on 88 cores.

insights.claim: Of the 8 integer/branch pipes, 2 are complex pipes handling multiply/divide plus specialized ops like CRCs and bit shifting; basic ALU operations complete in a single cycle, multiplies take as little as 2 cycles, and divides take between 5 and 20 cycles depending on iterations required.
insights.speaker: Ryan Smith
insights.so_what: Gives concrete latency figures useful for estimating real-world integer workload performance versus competing cores.

insights.claim: Olympus has more pipes for integer operations than floating point, meaning strong FP throughput depends on fully filling the 6 SVE2 vector pipes (up from 4 on Grace) with whole-vector operations; 2 of those vector pipes also handle cryptographic operations like AES and SHA.
insights.speaker: Ryan Smith
insights.so_what: Suggests Olympus's FP/vector performance will be more workload-dependent (vectorization-sensitive) than its integer performance, a nuance for benchmarking expectations.

insights.claim: Olympus's 4 load/store pipes split into 2 shared load-or-store pipes and 2 dedicated load-only pipes, and notably the design lacks dedicated address-generation pipes, which sets it apart from other contemporary architectures.
insights.speaker: Ryan Smith
insights.so_what: A specific architectural divergence from peers that could affect memory-bound workload performance in ways not yet fully understood.

themes.theme: Cache subsystem and the novel graph prefetcher

insights.claim: Olympus includes large TLBs, including a 3,000-entry second-level TLB, which is fairly common among high-end CPUs.
insights.speaker: Ryan Smith
insights.so_what: Confirms Olympus matches industry-standard TLB provisioning, not a differentiator but a baseline competence check.

insights.claim: NVIDIA implemented a 'novel' graph prefetcher designed to accelerate pointer-heavy, indirection-heavy graph-like data structures by identifying the producer-consumer relationship between a pointer and its destination and prefetching the target data before the pointer is even resolved -- and to the author's knowledge, neither AMD nor Intel has implemented a similar prefetcher, despite the idea circulating in academia for years.
insights.speaker: Ryan Smith
insights.so_what: A genuine architectural differentiator versus Intel and AMD that could give Olympus an edge on graph-analytics and pointer-chasing workloads common in AI and data-intensive applications.
insights.quote: neither AMD nor Intel have implemented a similar prefetcher, though the idea has been floating around academia for at least several years

themes.theme: First real performance signal: SPEC CPU 2026

insights.claim: NVIDIA's new SPEC CPU 2026 benchmark disclosures give the first real look at Vera's overall CPU performance, and the results are described as 'not all rosy' for NVIDIA, clearly showing where NVIDIA's core design priorities did and did not pay off.
insights.speaker: Ryan Smith
insights.so_what: A signal that despite the architectural sophistication detailed elsewhere in the piece, Vera's real-world benchmark performance has notable weaknesses worth investigating further before assuming competitive parity with Intel/AMD.
insights.quote: it is not all rosy for NVIDIA
